one_line_takeaway: A tutorial/perspective paper taxonomizing how Agentic AI can secure future 6G 'Intellicise' wireless networks and what new threats Agentic AI itself introduces, backed by one original but only qualitatively-evaluated case study of an LLM/diffusion-based coverless semantic steganography scheme.
key_finding: The paper's central qualitative claim, illustrated only by the Fig. 4 simulation, is that digital-token-controlled EDICT sampling makes the stego-image reconstruction cryptographically/generatively non-invertible for an eavesdropper lacking the correct token, even if that eavesdropper has obtained the public semantic key and implicit feature -- producing image collapse or misleading content instead of the true secret image. Beyond the case study, the paper's main organizing findings are the taxonomy itself (three domains x multiple sub-applications of Agentic AI for network security) and the two threat/defense catalogs (Table I: 12 threat types across 4 agent stages; Table II: 18 defense techniques across 3 categories), which are presented as a structured synthesis of the cited literature rather than as new empirical results.
core_contribution: A perspective/survey paper (IEEE-style, not an empirical study) that (1) proposes a structured taxonomy of Agentic AI applications for securing 'Intellicise' (Intelligent and Concise) 6G wireless networks, organized into three domains -- secure intellicise signal processing (wireless sensing, beamforming, RF fingerprint identification, radio map construction), secure intellicise information transmission (semantic encrypted, covert, and steganographic communication), and secure intellicise network organization (secure resource allocation, penetration testing, encrypted traffic detection); (2) catalogs, in two tables, the security/privacy threats Agentic AI itself introduces across its four operational stages (perception, memory, reasoning, action) and the corresponding defense technique categories (robustness enhancement, trustworthy design, real-time defense); (3) presents one original case study/proposed scheme -- an Agentic AI-based semantic steganography communication pipeline using LVM-based semantic extraction, LLM-generated public keys, ControlNet-conditioned reference-image generation, and EDICT-sampled diffusion for stego-image generation/recovery -- with qualitative simulation results (no quantitative metrics reported) on the UniStega dataset; and (4) lists three open research directions (secure/trustworthy Agentic AI network architecture, low-complexity Agentic AI model design, and platform/testbed establishment).
threat_model: Two distinct threat models are covered. (a) For the intellicise-network security applications (Section III), the threats are traditional wireless adversaries: eavesdroppers who capture CSI samples or intercept transmitted signals to infer private user activity, decode private semantic information, or exploit spatial channel correlation against beamforming; encrypted-traffic analysts; and network penetration testers simulating attackers. (b) For Agentic AI itself as an attack surface (Section IV, Table I), the paper enumerates threats mapped onto the perception-memory-reasoning-action loop: injection attacks and perception vulnerabilities (malicious multimodal instructions, intent-mapping failures), inference attacks (reverse-inferring internal agent states from behavior), localized mismatches, robot-detection false positives, memory poisoning of shared knowledge bases, reasoning-stage path hijacking/jailbreaks, objective-function corruption, multi-agent collusion, protocol-level attacks, and action-stage autonomy abuse, oversight saturation (audit-log flooding), and governance obfuscation (untraceable actions). For the case study specifically, the threat is an eavesdropper who intercepts the transmitted stego image and, even with access to the public semantic key and implicit feature, attempts to reconstruct the secret image without the correct digital token.
limitations: The paper does not state its own limitations explicitly, but grounded observations: (1) it is a perspective/tutorial-style paper, so the taxonomy and threat/defense tables are largely restatements of cited works rather than the authors' own experiments or novel analysis; (2) the sole original contribution (the steganography case study) reports no quantitative results -- no PSNR, SSIM, bit-error/recovery-accuracy numbers, or steganalysis-detection rates are given, only a single qualitative figure; (3) no baseline comparison is made against the prior coverless-steganography methods it builds on (e.g., DiffStega [14]) in the case study; (4) the threats-to-Agentic-AI table (Table I) and defenses table (Table II) are not validated against the intellicise wireless network case study itself, so it is unclear how effective the cataloged defenses (e.g., instruction hierarchy, audit log system) would be against the specific attacks described.

datasets_used: UniStega (used only in the Section V case study for qualitative visualization, not for the survey/taxonomy portions of the paper)
